flashcards.q: What are life processes?
flashcards.a: Basic maintenance functions like nutrition, respiration, transportation, and excretion that keep living organisms alive.

flashcards.q: Why is molecular movement necessary for life?
flashcards.a: To repair and maintain the organized structures of living organisms which break down over time.

flashcards.q: What are autotrophs?
flashcards.a: Organisms that synthesize food from inorganic sources like CO₂ and water using sunlight and chlorophyll.

flashcards.q: Which cell organelles contain chlorophyll?
flashcards.a: Chloroplasts.

flashcards.q: What is the function of stomata?
flashcards.a: Facilitate exchange of gases (CO₂ and O₂) and transpiration in plants.

flashcards.q: How do guard cells control the opening of stomatal pores?
flashcards.a: They swell when water flows into them, causing the pore to open; they shrink to close it.

flashcards.q: Name the biological catalysts that help in food breakdown.
flashcards.a: Enzymes.

flashcards.q: What is the role of salivary amylase?
flashcards.a: It breaks down complex starch into simple sugar in the mouth.

flashcards.q: Define 'peristalsis'.
flashcards.a: The rhythmic contraction and relaxation of muscles in the gut to push food forward.

flashcards.q: Why is HCl secreted in the stomach?
flashcards.a: To provide an acidic medium for pepsin and to kill harmful bacteria.

flashcards.q: What is the function of mucus in the stomach?
flashcards.a: To protect the inner lining of the stomach from the action of hydrochloric acid.

flashcards.q: Where does complete digestion of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats occur?
flashcards.a: In the small intestine.

flashcards.q: What are villi and what is their function?
flashcards.a: Finger-like projections in the small intestine that increase surface area for nutrient absorption.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between aerobic and anaerobic respiration?
flashcards.a: Aerobic respiration uses oxygen to release high energy; anaerobic respiration occurs without oxygen and releases less energy.

flashcards.q: What causes muscle cramps during sudden physical activity?
flashcards.a: The build-up of lactic acid in muscle cells due to anaerobic breakdown of pyruvate.

flashcards.q: What is the energy currency of the cell?
flashcards.a: ATP (Adenosine Triphosphate).

flashcards.q: In which part of the cell does the breakdown of glucose into pyruvate occur?
flashcards.a: In the cytoplasm.

flashcards.q: Why is the rate of breathing faster in aquatic organisms?
flashcards.a: Because the availability of dissolved oxygen in water is lower than that in air.

flashcards.q: What are alveoli?
flashcards.a: Balloon-like structures in the lungs that provide a large surface area for gas exchange.

flashcards.q: What is the role of haemoglobin in the human body?
flashcards.a: It is a respiratory pigment in Red Blood Corpsucles that carries oxygen from lungs to all tissues.

flashcards.q: How many chambers does a human heart have?
flashcards.a: Four chambers (two atria and two ventricles).

flashcards.q: What is double circulation?
flashcards.a: A system where blood passes through the heart twice in one complete cycle through the body.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between arteries and veins?
flashcards.a: Arteries carry high-pressure blood away from the heart (thick walls); veins carry blood back to the heart (thin walls, valves).

flashcards.q: What is lymph?
flashcards.a: A colourless fluid involved in transportation that carries absorbed fat and drains tissue fluid back to blood.

flashcards.q: What is the function of xylem and phloem in plants?
flashcards.a: Xylem transports water and minerals; phloem translocates food (sucrose) and amino acids.

flashcards.q: Define 'transpiration'.
flashcards.a: The loss of water in the form of vapour from the aerial parts of the plant.

flashcards.q: What is the structural and functional unit of the kidney?
flashcards.a: Nephron.

flashcards.q: What is hemodialysis?
flashcards.a: A process of using an artificial kidney to remove nitrogenous waste from the blood in case of kidney failure.

flashcards.q: How do plants store their waste products?
flashcards.a: In cellular vacuoles, in leaves that fall off, or as resins and gums in old xylem.
